answer:
Architecting a Scalable Todo App for 1M+ Users

Core Architecture Definition

A scalable Todo application at 1M+ user scale requires horizontal partitioning of data by user ID, event-driven state synchronization across devices, CQRS for separating read-heavy list views from write-heavy mutations, and a CDN-first frontend with optimistic UI updates. The system must handle ~50K concurrent WebSocket connections, ~200K todo mutations/hour, and sub-100ms list rendering for users with 10K+ items.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

In the HR Leave Management System at VisionQuest, Manoj built a task-like approval workflow where leave requests behave similarly to todo items — each has states (pending → approved/rejected), ownership, and real-time status updates via Socket.io. The 3-level approval chain mirrors a todo dependency graph. For ContactsHub, bulk CSV upload (500/batch) demonstrated handling large data ingestion pipelines — the same pattern applies to bulk todo import. The CRM Dashboard's lazy-loaded Angular modules informed how to partition UI for a todo app with categories, tags, and search.

System Architecture

Implementation: Sharded Todo Repository + Optimistic UI

// Backend: Fastify + Prisma sharded todo service
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const shards = [
  new PrismaClient({ datasources: { db: { url: process.env.SHARD_0_URL } } }),
  new PrismaClient({ datasources: { db: { url: process.env.SHARD_1_URL } } }),
  new PrismaClient({ datasources: { db: { url: process.env.SHARD_2_URL } } }),
];

function getShard(userId: string) {
  const hash = userId.split('').reduce((a, c) => a + c.charCodeAt(0), 0);
  return shards[hash % shards.length];
}

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });

// Create todo with event emission
app.post('/todos', async (req, reply) => {
  const { userId, title, dueDate, priority } = req.body;
  const db = getShard(userId);
  
  const todo = await db.todo.create({
    data: { userId, title, dueDate: new Date(dueDate), priority, status: 'pending' }
  });
  
  // Publish to Kafka for real-time sync
  await kafkaProducer.send({
    topic: 'todo-events',
    messages: [{ key: userId, value: JSON.stringify({ type: 'TODO_CREATED', todo }) }]
  });
  
  // Invalidate Redis cache
  await redis.del(`todos:${userId}:list`);
  
  return reply.status(201).send(todo);
});

// Read with Redis cache layer
app.get('/todos/:userId', async (req, reply) => {
  const { userId } = req.params;
  const cached = await redis.get(`todos:${userId}:list`);
  if (cached) return reply.send(JSON.parse(cached));
  
  const db = getShard(userId);
  const todos = await db.todo.findMany({
    where: { userId },
    orderBy: [{ priority: 'desc' }, { dueDate: 'asc' }],
    take: 100 // Paginated
  });
  
  await redis.setex(`todos:${userId}:list`, 300, JSON.stringify(todos));
  return reply.send(todos);
});

// Frontend: React optimistic update hook
function useTodoMutation() {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();
  
  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: (newTodo) => fetch('/api/todos', {
      method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(newTodo)
    }).then(r => r.json()),
    
    onMutate: async (newTodo) => {
      await queryClient.cancelQueries(['todos']);
      const previous = queryClient.getQueryData(['todos']);
      queryClient.setQueryData(['todos'], (old) => [
        { ...newTodo, id: 'temp-' + Date.now(), status: 'pending' },
        ...old
      ]);
      return { previous };
    },
    
    onError: (err, newTodo, context) => {
      queryClient.setQueryData(['todos'], context.previous);
      toast.error('Failed to create todo — rolled back');
    },
    
    onSettled: () => queryClient.invalidateQueries(['todos'])
  });
}

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the HR Leave System, Manoj encountered a scenario similar to todo list scaling when the leave calendar had to render 500+ leave entries simultaneously. The Angular component froze for 3 seconds on load. He profiled with Chrome DevTools and found *ngFor was re-rendering all 500 rows on every change detection cycle. The fix was implementing trackBy: trackByLeaveId and switching to OnPush change detection, dropping render time to 180ms. For a todo app at 1M+ users, the same principle applies: virtualized lists (react-window) are mandatory — rendering only visible items keeps DOM nodes under 50 regardless of list size. In ContactsHub, he applied the same virtualization pattern when the contacts list exceeded 2,000 entries after bulk CSV import.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Single database for all usersQuery latency > 2s at 1M usersShard by user_id; keep all user data co-located
No cache invalidation strategyStale todo lists, missed updatesRedis TTL 300s + explicit invalidation on mutation
WebSocket connection limitServer crashes at 50K connectionsHorizontal WebSocket pods with Redis Pub/Sub backplane
Unbounded list renderingBrowser tab crashes with 10K+ todosVirtual scrolling + cursor-based pagination (not offset)
No offline supportUsers lose work on connectivity dropsIndexedDB queue + conflict resolution on reconnect

Validation Strategy

  • Load test: k6 script simulating 100K concurrent users creating/reading todos — target p99 < 200ms
  • Shard rebalancing: Verify consistent hashing redistributes < 10% data on node addition
  • Cache hit ratio: Monitor Redis hit rate > 85% for read endpoints
  • WebSocket reconnection: Playwright test: kill connection, verify auto-reconnect < 3s with no data loss
  • Optimistic UI rollback: Simulate 500 error, verify UI reverts to previous state within 1 render cycle

Performance Bottlenecks in Todo App — Instrumentation & Resolution

Core Bottleneck Categories

Todo app performance bottlenecks at scale cluster into four domains: Database query amplification (N+1 queries when loading todos with tags/subtasks), real-time sync overhead (fan-out explosion when shared lists update), frontend rendering stalls (DOM thrashing with large lists), and cache stampede (concurrent cache misses triggering thundering herd on DB). Instrumentation requires distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), Real User Monitoring (RUM), and database query analysis with pg_stat_statements.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

At VisionQuest, the CRM Dashboard experienced classic N+1 queries when loading sales pipeline cards — each card fetched its associated contacts separately. Manoj instrumented with Express middleware logging query counts per request, discovered 47 queries for a 15-card view, and fixed it with MongoDB $lookup aggregation (equivalent to SQL JOINs). In the HR Leave System, Socket.io broadcast storms occurred when 50+ users were on the dashboard simultaneously — every leave status change triggered 50 WebSocket emissions. He introduced room-based namespacing so updates only reached affected department members, cutting WebSocket messages by 80%.

Bottleneck Map & Instrumentation Points

Instrumentation & Fix Implementation

// 1. OpenTelemetry tracing for Fastify todo routes
import { trace } from '@opentelemetry/api';

const tracer = trace.getTracer('todo-service');

app.get('/todos/:userId', async (req, reply) => {
  const span = tracer.startSpan('GET /todos/:userId');
  span.setAttribute('user.id', req.params.userId);
  
  try {
    // Instrument DB query time
    const dbSpan = tracer.startSpan('db.findMany', { parent: span });
    const todos = await db.todo.findMany({
      where: { userId: req.params.userId },
      include: { tags: true, subtasks: true }, // Eager load — kills N+1
      orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' },
      take: 50
    });
    dbSpan.setAttribute('db.row_count', todos.length);
    dbSpan.end();
    
    span.setStatus({ code: 0 });
    return reply.send(todos);
  } catch (err) {
    span.setStatus({ code: 2, message: err.message });
    throw err;
  } finally {
    span.end();
  }
});

// 2. Singleflight cache to prevent thundering herd
const inflight = new Map();

async function singleflightGet(key, fetcher) {
  if (inflight.has(key)) return inflight.get(key);
  
  const promise = (async () => {
    const cached = await redis.get(key);
    if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);
    
    const data = await fetcher();
    await redis.setex(key, 300, JSON.stringify(data));
    return data;
  })();
  
  inflight.set(key, promise);
  try { return await promise; }
  finally { inflight.delete(key); }
}

// 3. Frontend: web-vitals instrumentation
import { onLCP, onFID, onCLS } from 'web-vitals';

function reportMetric(metric) {
  fetch('/api/metrics', {
    method: 'POST',
    body: JSON.stringify({
      name: metric.name, value: metric.value,
      page: window.location.pathname,
      timestamp: Date.now()
    })
  });
}

onLCP(reportMetric);  // Largest Contentful Paint — todo list render
onFID(reportMetric);  // First Input Delay — checkbox toggle latency
onCLS(reportMetric);  // Cumulative Layout Shift — list reflow

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the HR Leave System, Manoj discovered a cache stampede when the admin dashboard was accessed by 30 managers simultaneously at 9 AM. The Redis cache for the team leave summary had expired, and all 30 requests hit PostgreSQL concurrently, causing a 12-second spike visible in pg_stat_activity. He implemented the singleflight pattern shown above — wrapping the cache lookup in a deduplication layer so only one DB query executed while others awaited the same promise. This dropped the 9 AM spike from 12s to 400ms. The same pattern directly applies to todo list cache stampedes when popular shared lists expire simultaneously.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Tracing every request in production30% overhead on p99 latencySample at 10% in prod; 100% only for error traces
N+1 queries on tags/subtasks47 queries per list load → 800msPrisma include for eager loading; DataLoader for GraphQL
Cache stampede on popular listsDB connection pool exhaustedSingleflight + stale-while-revalidate pattern
Unindexed search queriesFull-table scan on text searchGIN index for PostgreSQL full-text; Elasticsearch for complex queries
Ignoring CLS from async data loadsPoor Core Web Vitals scoreSkeleton placeholders with fixed dimensions; content-visibility: auto

Validation Strategy

  • APM dashboard: Grafana panels showing p50/p95/p99 for each todo endpoint; alert on p99 > 500ms
  • Query analysis: Weekly pg_stat_statements review; flag queries with mean_exec_time > 100ms
  • Load test baseline: k6 with 10K VUs — verify throughput > 5K RPS with p99 < 300ms
  • RUM correlation: Cross-reference web-vitals data with server traces to identify frontend vs backend bottlenecks
  • Cache effectiveness: Redis INFO stats — hit ratio > 90%, eviction rate < 1%/min

Architecting a Scalable Auth Module for 1M+ Users

Core Architecture Definition

A scalable authentication module at 1M+ users requires stateless JWT tokens with short-lived access tokens (15min) and long-lived refresh tokens (7d) stored in a dedicated token store (Redis), rate-limited login endpoints to prevent credential stuffing, distributed session management for token revocation, and OAuth2/OIDC federation for enterprise SSO. The system must handle ~10K logins/minute at peak, support MFA without latency degradation, and provide instant token revocation across all nodes.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented the complete JWT auth flow with Fastify and PostgreSQL/Prisma: access tokens (15-min expiry) issued on login, refresh token rotation on each renewal, and role-based middleware (Employee → Manager → HR Admin). The 3-level approval chain required granular permission checks at every API endpoint. For ContactsHub, he built JWT auth with Fastify + MongoDB and added persistent sessions for PWA offline mode, where tokens were cached in IndexedDB and validated on reconnect.

Scalable Auth Architecture

Implementation: JWT Auth with Refresh Token Rotation

// Fastify auth plugin with token rotation
import fastifyJwt from '@fastify/jwt';
import argon2 from 'argon2';
import { randomUUID } from 'crypto';

app.register(fastifyJwt, { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET });

// Login with rate limiting
app.post('/auth/login', {
  config: { rateLimit: { max: 100, timeWindow: '15 minutes' } }
}, async (req, reply) => {
  const { email, password } = req.body;
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
  if (!user || !(await argon2.verify(user.passwordHash, password))) {
    return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  }
  
  // Issue token pair
  const accessToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, role: user.role },
    { expiresIn: '15m' }
  );
  
  const refreshFamily = randomUUID();
  const refreshToken = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: user.id, family: refreshFamily, version: 1 },
    { expiresIn: '7d' }
  );
  
  // Store refresh token family in Redis
  await redis.setex(
    `refresh:${refreshFamily}`,
    7 * 24 * 3600,
    JSON.stringify({ userId: user.id, version: 1 })
  );
  
  reply.send({ accessToken, refreshToken });
});

// Refresh with rotation & family-based theft detection
app.post('/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const { refreshToken } = req.body;
  const decoded = app.jwt.verify(refreshToken);
  
  const stored = await redis.get(`refresh:${decoded.family}`);
  if (!stored) return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Token family revoked' });
  
  const familyData = JSON.parse(stored);
  
  // Detect token reuse (theft indicator)
  if (familyData.version !== decoded.version) {
    await redis.del(`refresh:${decoded.family}`); // Revoke entire family
    // Alert security monitoring
    logger.warn(`Token reuse detected for user ${decoded.sub}`);
    return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Token reuse detected' });
  }
  
  // Issue new pair with incremented version
  const newAccess = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: decoded.sub, role: familyData.role },
    { expiresIn: '15m' }
  );
  const newRefresh = app.jwt.sign(
    { sub: decoded.sub, family: decoded.family, version: decoded.version + 1 },
    { expiresIn: '7d' }
  );
  
  await redis.setex(
    `refresh:${decoded.family}`,
    7 * 24 * 3600,
    JSON.stringify({ ...familyData, version: decoded.version + 1 })
  );
  
  reply.send({ accessToken: newAccess, refreshToken: newRefresh });
});

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the HR Leave System, Manoj discovered that JWT tokens were surviving logout — users could copy a token before logging out and reuse it. The root cause was that logout only cleared the client-side cookie but didn't invalidate the token server-side. He implemented a Redis blacklist: on logout, the remaining TTL of the access token was calculated and stored in Redis with that TTL. The auth middleware checked the blacklist before processing any request. This added ~2ms per request (Redis GET) but ensured instant revocation. At scale, the blacklist uses minimal memory since entries auto-expire with the token TTL.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
bcrypt instead of argon2 for hashingVulnerable to GPU-accelerated attacksUse argon2id with memory=64MB, iterations=3
Long-lived access tokens (24h+)Stolen tokens valid for hours15-min access tokens + refresh rotation
No rate limiting on loginCredential stuffing at 10K req/sec100 attempts/15min/IP + CAPTCHA after 5 failures
Storing refresh tokens in localStorageXSS can steal refresh tokensHttpOnly Secure SameSite=Strict cookies
Single JWT secret across servicesOne compromised service = all compromisedRS256 with per-service key pairs; rotate every 90 days

Validation Strategy

  • Credential stuffing test: Run 1K login attempts/min from single IP — verify rate limiter blocks after 100
  • Token rotation test: Use same refresh token twice — verify family revocation triggers
  • Revocation latency: Logout user, immediately call protected endpoint — verify 401 within 50ms
  • Concurrent auth load: k6 test with 10K concurrent logins — verify p99 < 500ms (argon2 is CPU-bound)
  • Key rotation: Rotate JWT signing key — verify existing tokens still validate during grace period

Performance Bottlenecks in Auth Module — Instrumentation & Resolution

Core Bottleneck Categories

Auth module bottlenecks at scale include: CPU-bound password hashing (argon2/bcrypt blocking the event loop), Redis hot-key contention on token store operations, database connection exhaustion during login spikes (Monday 9 AM), and JWT validation overhead when verifying RS256 signatures on every request. The critical constraint is that auth sits on the hot path — every API call passes through token verification, so even 10ms overhead compounds to significant latency at 50K RPS.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

In the HR Leave System, Manoj observed that login requests during the Monday 9 AM rush (when ~200 employees logged in simultaneously) caused Fastify response times to spike from 50ms to 2.8 seconds. Profiling revealed that argon2.verify() was CPU-bound — each verification took ~300ms, and Node.js's single thread couldn't parallelize. He moved password verification to a worker thread pool using piscina, allowing 4 concurrent hash verifications without blocking the event loop. In ContactsHub, he noticed JWT verification was happening on every Socket.io message, adding 5ms per message. He cached decoded tokens in a WeakMap keyed by the raw token string.

Auth Bottleneck Timeline & Fixes

Implementation: Worker Pool + JWT Cache

// 1. Offload argon2 to worker threads with piscina
import Piscina from 'piscina';
import { resolve } from 'path';

const hashPool = new Piscina({
  filename: resolve(__dirname, 'hash-worker.js'),
  minThreads: 2,
  maxThreads: Math.max(1, require('os').cpus().length - 1)
});

// hash-worker.js
import argon2 from 'argon2';
export default async ({ hash, password }) => {
  return argon2.verify(hash, password);
};

// Login handler — non-blocking
app.post('/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email: req.body.email } });
  if (!user) return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  
  // Offload to worker thread — event loop stays free
  const valid = await hashPool.run({ hash: user.passwordHash, password: req.body.password });
  if (!valid) return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });
  
  const token = app.jwt.sign({ sub: user.id, role: user.role }, { expiresIn: '15m' });
  return reply.send({ accessToken: token });
});

// 2. JWT verification cache — avoid re-parsing same token
const tokenCache = new Map(); // Use LRU in production

function cachedVerify(token) {
  if (tokenCache.has(token)) {
    const cached = tokenCache.get(token);
    if (cached.exp * 1000 > Date.now()) return cached;
    tokenCache.delete(token);
  }
  const decoded = app.jwt.verify(token);
  tokenCache.set(token, decoded);
  // Prevent memory leak: cap at 10K entries
  if (tokenCache.size > 10000) {
    const firstKey = tokenCache.keys().next().value;
    tokenCache.delete(firstKey);
  }
  return decoded;
}

// 3. Prisma connection pool monitoring
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
const prisma = new PrismaClient({
  datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL + '?connection_limit=20&pool_timeout=10' } },
  log: [{ level: 'warn', emit: 'event' }]
});

prisma.$on('warn', (e) => {
  if (e.message.includes('pool')) {
    metrics.increment('db.pool.warning');
    logger.warn('DB connection pool pressure:', e.message);
  }
});

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the HR Leave System at VisionQuest, the Monday 9 AM login spike caused a cascading failure: 200 concurrent logins meant 200 argon2 verifications queued on the single event loop, each taking ~300ms. By login #50, the Fastify server stopped responding to health checks and the load balancer marked it as unhealthy, causing a restart loop. Manoj diagnosed this by adding process.hrtime() measurements around each async boundary and found that the event loop lag exceeded 5 seconds during the spike. The fix was the piscina worker pool shown above — argon2 now runs in parallel across 3 worker threads while the event loop remains free to handle health checks and other requests. Post-fix, the Monday 9 AM spike showed p99 login time of 180ms with zero dropped health checks.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
argon2 on main event loopEvent loop blocked 300ms/requestWorker thread pool (piscina) — 4 parallel verifications
Unbounded JWT verification cacheMemory leak over timeLRU cache with max 10K entries; TTL aligned to token expiry
DB connection pool too smallConnection timeout during login spikespool_size=20 + pool_timeout=10s + monitoring alerts
RS256 verification on every request5ms overhead × 50K RPS = bottleneckHS256 for internal services; RS256 only for external-facing tokens
Redis single-key hot spotRate limiter key per IP gets hotUse sliding window with hash slots; distribute across Redis cluster

Validation Strategy

  • Event loop lag monitor: perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay() — alert when p99 > 100ms
  • Login spike simulation: k6 ramp-up 0→500 logins/sec — verify no health check failures
  • Worker pool saturation: Monitor piscina.queueSize — alert when > 50 pending tasks
  • Connection pool utilization: Track active/idle connections — alert at 80% pool usage
  • Token cache hit ratio: Must be > 70% for read-heavy workloads; < 50% indicates short-lived tokens being wasted

Architecting a Scalable Pagination API for 1M+ Users

Core Architecture Definition

Scalable pagination at 1M+ users demands cursor-based pagination over offset-based — OFFSET 100000 forces the database to scan and discard 100K rows before returning results. Cursor pagination uses an indexed column (e.g., WHERE created_at < :cursor ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50) achieving O(log n) seeks regardless of page depth. For real-time feeds where items are inserted/deleted frequently, a keyset cursor combining (created_at, id) prevents duplicate/missed items. At 1M+ users, the API must also support cached cursors, consistent pagination under concurrent writes, and response envelope standardization for client-side infinite scroll.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj built the sales pipeline view with paginated lead lists. Initially using offset pagination (.skip(page * 20).limit(20) in MongoDB), performance degraded visibly when the sales team accumulated 15K+ leads. Page 500 took 3.2 seconds. He migrated to cursor-based pagination using _id as the cursor, dropping response time to a consistent 45ms regardless of page depth. In the HR Leave System, the leave history view paginated leave records by created_at with a composite cursor (created_at, leave_id) to handle ties — multiple leaves submitted at the same timestamp.

Offset vs Cursor Pagination Performance

Implementation: Cursor-Based Pagination with Fastify + Prisma

// Fastify cursor pagination endpoint
import { z } from 'zod';

const CursorSchema = z.object({
  cursor: z.string().optional(),
  limit: z.number().min(1).max(100).default(50),
  direction: z.enum(['forward', 'backward']).default('forward')
});

app.get('/api/todos', async (req, reply) => {
  const { cursor, limit, direction } = CursorSchema.parse(req.query);
  
  // Decode cursor: base64(JSON.stringify({ createdAt, id }))
  let cursorData = null;
  if (cursor) {
    cursorData = JSON.parse(Buffer.from(cursor, 'base64url').toString());
  }
  
  const where = cursorData ? {
    OR: [
      { createdAt: { [direction === 'forward' ? 'lt' : 'gt']: new Date(cursorData.createdAt) } },
      {
        createdAt: new Date(cursorData.createdAt),
        id: { [direction === 'forward' ? 'lt' : 'gt']: cursorData.id }
      }
    ]
  } : {};
  
  const items = await prisma.todo.findMany({
    where: { userId: req.user.sub, ...where },
    orderBy: [
      { createdAt: direction === 'forward' ? 'desc' : 'asc' },
      { id: direction === 'forward' ? 'desc' : 'asc' }
    ],
    take: limit + 1 // Fetch one extra to detect hasMore
  });
  
  const hasMore = items.length > limit;
  const data = hasMore ? items.slice(0, limit) : items;
  
  const nextCursor = hasMore ? Buffer.from(
    JSON.stringify({ createdAt: data[data.length - 1].createdAt, id: data[data.length - 1].id })
  ).toString('base64url') : null;
  
  // Cache the response for 30s
  reply.header('Cache-Control', 'private, max-age=30');
  
  return {
    data,
    cursor: { next: nextCursor },
    hasMore,
    totalEstimate: await prisma.todo.count({ where: { userId: req.user.sub } })
  };
});

// Frontend: React infinite scroll with cursor pagination
function useTodoList() {
  return useInfiniteQuery({
    queryKey: ['todos'],
    queryFn: async ({ pageParam }) => {
      const url = pageParam
        ? `/api/todos?cursor=${pageParam}&limit=50`
        : '/api/todos?limit=50';
      return fetch(url).then(r => r.json());
    },
    getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.cursor.next ?? undefined,
    staleTime: 30_000
  });
}

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the CRM Dashboard, after migrating from offset to cursor pagination, users reported seeing duplicate leads when scrolling. The root cause: the cursor was based solely on created_at, but bulk lead imports created 50 leads with identical timestamps. When the cursor pointed to a created_at value shared by 50 records, the next page could repeat some. Manoj fixed this by using a composite cursor of (created_at, _id) — since MongoDB's _id is globally unique, ties are always broken deterministically. He validated the fix by writing a Playwright test that scrolled through 500 leads and asserted zero duplicate IDs in the rendered list.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
OFFSET pagination at depthO(n) scan — 5s at page 10KCursor-based pagination with indexed columns
Single-column cursor with tiesDuplicate/missing items on page boundariesComposite cursor (timestamp + unique ID)
COUNT(*) for total on every requestFull table scan on large datasetsEstimate via pg_class.reltuples or cache count with TTL
No cursor validationInjection via crafted cursor stringsSign cursors with HMAC; validate on decode
Cache invalidation on new itemsNew items invisible until cache expiresInvalidate first-page cache on insert; subsequent pages are stable

Validation Strategy

  • Deep page benchmark: k6 test fetching page 1 vs page 10K — verify < 10% latency difference
  • Duplicate detection: Playwright scroll test collecting all rendered IDs — assert uniqueness across 1K items
  • Concurrent write stability: Insert 100 items while paginating — verify no items skipped or duplicated
  • Cursor tampering: Send modified base64 cursor — verify 400 Bad Request, not server error
  • Cache coherence: Insert new item, verify it appears on first page within 30s cache TTL

Performance Bottlenecks in Pagination API — Instrumentation & Resolution

Core Bottleneck Categories

Pagination API bottlenecks at scale include: COUNT(*) queries that require full table scans for total counts, missing composite indexes causing sequential scans on cursor columns, large payload serialization when items include nested relations, and memory pressure from holding large result sets during serialization. The critical insight is that pagination queries run on the hottest path — every list view, every scroll event — so even 50ms overhead per page translates to noticeable UX degradation for infinite scroll.

Manoj's Real-World Mapping

In the CRM Dashboard, Manoj discovered that the lead list endpoint was the slowest in the entire API — 68% of all API calls were paginated list views. MongoDB's explain() showed the query was doing a COLLSCAN instead of using the index because the sort order ({ updatedAt: -1 }) didn't match the existing index ({ createdAt: 1 }). Adding a compound index { updatedAt: -1, _id: -1 } dropped p95 from 800ms to 35ms. In the HR Leave System, the leave history endpoint returned full leave objects with nested approver details — a select clause in Prisma to return only display fields reduced payload size from 4KB to 800 bytes per item.

Pagination Bottleneck Diagnosis Flow

Implementation: Optimized Pagination with Instrumentation

// 1. Prisma query with proper indexing and field selection
// schema.prisma — compound index for cursor pagination
// @@index([createdAt(sort: Desc), id(sort: Desc)])

app.get('/api/items', async (req, reply) => {
  const start = performance.now();
  const { cursor, limit = 50 } = req.query;
  
  const items = await prisma.item.findMany({
    where: cursor ? {
      OR: [
        { createdAt: { lt: new Date(decodeCursor(cursor).createdAt) } },
        {
          createdAt: new Date(decodeCursor(cursor).createdAt),
          id: { lt: decodeCursor(cursor).id }
        }
      ],
      userId: req.user.sub
    } : { userId: req.user.sub },
    orderBy: [{ createdAt: 'desc' }, { id: 'desc' }],
    take: limit + 1,
    // Field selection — only return what the list view needs
    select: {
      id: true, title: true, status: true,
      createdAt: true, priority: true,
      _count: { select: { subtasks: true } } // Count, not full objects
    }
  });
  
  const duration = performance.now() - start;
  metrics.histogram('pagination.query_ms', duration);
  metrics.histogram('pagination.items_returned', Math.min(items.length, limit));
  
  if (duration > 200) {
    logger.warn(`Slow pagination query: ${duration}ms`, {
      cursor, limit, userId: req.user.sub
    });
  }
  
  const hasMore = items.length > limit;
  const data = hasMore ? items.slice(0, limit) : items;
  
  return { data, cursor: { next: hasMore ? encodeCursor(data.at(-1)) : null }, hasMore };
});

// 2. Estimated count instead of exact COUNT(*)
async function estimatedCount(table) {
  const cacheKey = `count:${table}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey);
  if (cached) return parseInt(cached);
  
  // Use pg_class for O(1) estimate
  const result = await prisma.$queryRaw`
    SELECT reltuples::bigint AS estimate
    FROM pg_class WHERE relname = ${table}`;
  
  const estimate = Number(result[0]?.estimate || 0);
  await redis.setex(cacheKey, 60, estimate.toString());
  return estimate;
}

// 3. Response compression for large payloads
app.register(import('@fastify/compress'), {
  threshold: 1024, // Compress responses > 1KB
  encodings: ['gzip', 'br']
});

Manoj's Debugging War Story

In the HR Leave System, the admin's "All Leave History" page was loading in 4.5 seconds. Manoj added prisma.$on('query') logging and discovered two issues: (1) the query included a COUNT(*) for the total — scanning 50K rows took 2.1 seconds, and (2) each leave record included the full approver relation with 15 fields when the list only displayed the approver name. He replaced the exact count with a cached estimate (refreshed every 60 seconds), and added a select clause to return only 6 fields per leave record. Combined, these changes dropped the endpoint from 4.5s to 120ms. He also added a Fastify onResponse hook that logged response payload sizes, revealing that the unoptimized endpoint was returning 380KB per page — after field selection, it dropped to 28KB.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Missing compound index on cursor columnsSequential scan — O(n) per pageCREATE INDEX idx ON items(created_at DESC, id DESC)
Exact COUNT(*) on every page requestFull table scan for total countpg_class estimate + cached count with 60s TTL
Returning full objects with nested relations380KB payloads, slow serializationPrisma select clause — only list view fields
No response compressionDouble bandwidth usage on mobile@fastify/compress with Brotli for modern browsers
No pagination query monitoringSlow queries go unnoticed until users complainHistogram metric + alert on p95 > 200ms

Validation Strategy

  • EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Run paginated query at depth 1K — verify Index Scan (not Seq Scan)
  • Payload size check: Assert response size < 50KB for 50-item pages
  • Count accuracy: Compare pg_class estimate vs exact count — must be within 5% for tables > 10K rows
  • Latency consistency: Benchmark page 1 vs page 1K — must be within 20% latency
  • Compression ratio: Verify Brotli achieves > 70% compression on JSON list responses
